sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Pregnancy-related pelvic girdle pain is common and can markedly affect walking, bed mobility, stairs, and work function. Management is usually conservative but should still screen for obstetric or neurological red flags.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain around the posterior pelvis, pubic symphysis, buttock, or groin
- Pain with rolling in bed, stairs, single-leg tasks, or getting in and out of vehicles
- Waddling gait or reduced walking tolerance
- Sleep disturbance and reduced ability to work or care for self

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for bleeding, reduced fetal movements, fever, neurological deficit, trauma, inability to weight-bear, or suspicion of non-MSK abdominal pathology
- Symptom severity does not always correlate with imaging findings
- Practical activity modification often matters more than aggressive manual treatment

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen obstetric red flags, recent trauma, and safe mobility
- Advise pacing, shorter stride, reduced asymmetrical loading, and supported transfers
- Escalate if pain is severe enough to prevent safe mobility or if non-MSK features are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess function, movement triggers, and tolerance to work and daily tasks
- Use belt discussion, positional advice, graded exercise, and load modification where appropriate
- Coordinate obstetric or medical review when pain is severe, atypical, or not behaving like straightforward PGP

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Goals should focus on walking, bed mobility, transfers, and work participation rather than pain scores alone
